# Statute of Limitations

A statute of limitations sets the maximum time after an event within which legal
proceedings may be started. Once the period expires, a claim is generally barred even
if it would otherwise have succeeded. The limits exist so that disputes are resolved
while evidence is still fresh and people are not left exposed to stale claims forever.

## How the clock runs

The length of the period depends on the type of claim, with serious crimes sometimes
having no limit at all. The clock usually starts when the harm occurs or is
discovered, and it can sometimes be paused, for example while a claimant is a minor.
Because the rules vary widely between places and claim types, missing a deadline is a
common and costly mistake.
